chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 CRU International Limited confidential Executive Summary What will happen to the market surplus?  The cobalt market is currently being impacted by oversupply and slower than expected demand. Prices of low grade material contracted by 12.3% year-on-year to US$16.44/lb in 2011 and a further 18.5% year-on-year to US$14.43/lb in H1 2012. CRU forecasts that prices will average US$14.00/lb in full year 2012 due to a combination of refined surplus along with high levels of inventories of raw materials in China. Looking ahead, we predict that consecutive annual surpluses in the refined market will continue weighing on prices in the medium-term. It is anticipated that prices will reach a low of US$12.80/lb in 2014, before increasing to US$17.00/lb in 2017, when the refined market is predicted to turn to a deficit.  Despite a slower than expected consumption growth in the near-term, we anticipate that demand for refined cobalt will register a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022. This is largely driven by demand for cobalt oxide used in rechargeable batteries. Other sectors such as aerospace and advanced engineering are projected to support robust demand growth.

chunk.text: 2 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Executive Summary CRU International Limited confidential Current market: Oversupply concerns weigh on prices The prevailing sentiment in the cobalt market is one characterised by oversupply concerns. Even though demand has been expanding at a CAGR of 6.0% in the last 10 years, capacity additions mainly triggered by copper and nickel projects have added a significant amount of cobalt units, thus putting downward pressure on prices. The above, coupled with macroeconomic challenges, generates a bearish feeling in the cobalt market. Since mid-2011, this negative feeling has been exacerbated by a slowdown in China; nevertheless, cobalt consumption is growing strongly but at a slower rate than projected last year. Benchmark 99.3% prices contracted by 12.3% to US$16.44/lb in 2011. In H1 2012, prices registered further losses to US$14.43/lb, an 18.5% decline from H1 2011. CRU forecasts that prices will average US$14.00/lb in 2012; at the time of writing, 99.3% material was trading below US$13.00/lb. Oversupply concerns have been weighing on prices. We estimate that the refined cobalt market was balanced in 2010 and exhibited a surplus of 2,781 tonnes in 2011.

relations.evidence: Benchmark 99.3% prices contracted by 12.3% to US$16.44/lb in 2011.

chunk.text: ncerns have been weighing on prices. We estimate that the refined cobalt market was balanced in 2010 and exhibited a surplus of 2,781 tonnes in 2011. For the current year, we predict that the surplus will fall to 420 tonnes. This is mainly attributed to the fact that small Chinese processors are expected to cut primary production in the current year. However, the predicted fall in the market surplus for 2012 does not imply that the cobalt market will be tighter. This is because there is a significant accumulation of concentrates and intermediates stocks; we estimate that those stocks in China amount to about 17,000 tonnes of cobalt contained. We believe that the raw material stockpile in China is another factor putting downward pressure on market prices. Those inventories are expected to fall in order to meet a deficit in the raw material market in 2011-2013. In addition, there is evidence from trade statistics that Chinese imports of cobalt feed have recently fallen substantially. While it is more obvious to assess market tightness by looking at the refined market balance, CRU is of the opinion that the cobalt raw material market also has an important role.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Executive Summary 3 CRU International Limited confidential Future market: Supply shortages are expected in the long-term The current oversupply concerns extend to the medium-term as well. While refined demand is anticipated to expand at a CAGR of 6.8% in 2012-2017, supply growth is expected to be faster. Taking a conservative approach in our supply forecasts, we predict that the refined cobalt market will see consecutive annual surpluses in 2013-2016. We believe that the refined market surplus will reach 4,173 tonnes in 2014; benchmark prices are predicted to contract to US$12.80/lb in that year. The refined market is projected to turn to a deficit in 2017; prices are forecast to increase to US$17.00/lb. CRU would like to emphasise the fact that our refined production forecasts are backed by sufficient supply of raw materials. Our detailed raw material supply projection out to 2017 shows that there will be sufficient feed to support refined production. In fact, due to significant capacity additions of intermediate products, total raw material supply growth is expected to be enough in order to meet refined production needs out to 2017.

relations.evidence: Our detailed raw material supply projection out to 2017 shows that there will be sufficient feed to support refined production.

constraints.condition: refined market is projected to turn to a deficit in 2017; prices are forecast to increase to US$17.00/lb

chunk.text: additions of intermediate products, total raw material supply growth is expected to be enough in order to meet refined production needs out to 2017. CRU believes that raw material supply growth will be mainly a result of robust growth in the supply of intermediate products, rather than concentrates. It is anticipated that the supply of concentrates will fall. The DRC intends to process concentrates domestically, in order to capture the value added and export feed in the form of intermediates. This trend is justified by recent trade statistics and CRU believes that it will continue in the medium-term. However, the cobalt market is anticipated to see a turning point in the long-run. With demand expected to continue growing robustly, we believe that there is going to be a shortage of cobalt units. Even though in the near/medium-term the cobalt market is expected to be oversupplied mainly due to significant copper/nickel-triggered capacity additions, the picture changes dramatically in the long- term. Looking at the potential of new projects, we understand that the outlook for further additions is uncertain.

From what we identify as active new projects, 75% of them are still at the exploration and development stage. Medium-term oversupply concerns and low current prices have affected the development of new projects. This has also been impacted by a weak macroeconomic environment. A long-term shortage of cobalt units is based on the prediction that refined demand will have grown at a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022. This growth rate is largely a function of consumption trends, especially in the critical field of rechargeable batteries. As the size of the total cobalt market grows, the additional quantity demanded each year by end-use sectors increases as well. For instance, in the period 2001-2010, demand for refined cobalt grew at an average of 3,228 tonnes per annum. However, according to CRU’s projections the equivalent volume in 2011-2020 is anticipated to soar to 6,021 tonnes. In practice this is about the size of one project alone.

chunk.text: 4 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Executive Summary CRU International Limited confidential Demand: Robust growth anticipated in the medium/long-term Refined cobalt consumption continued to grow robustly in 2011, registering a 5.9% year-on-year increase to 77,015 tonnes. That was a return to long-term growth rates, down from a 13.9% expansion in 2010. Our estimates suggest that cobalt demand growth is strong and consistently outperforms advances in the global industrial production index. Demand from chemical sectors reached 44,150 tonnes in 2011, a 6.3% increase from the previous year. That was primarily driven by cobalt oxide used in lithium ion battery cells. It was also supported by demand for cobalt oxide, which is used in the manufacture of PET. CRU introduced a new end-use sector for cobalt sulphate; we identified that copper production used 471 tonnes of cobalt contained in 2011. Demand from metallurgical sectors rose by 5.3% year-on-year to 32,865 tonnes in 2011. Cobalt used in cemented carbides and rotating components for aircraft engines saw strong growth, boosted by healthy demand from the engineering and aerospace sectors respectively.

It is anticipated that refined cobalt consumption will increase by 5.9% year-on-year to 81,554 tonnes in 2012. Despite a weaker than previously expected economic outlook, CRU believes that demand for cobalt will benefit from high value added and growing end-use sectors. Expanding markets such as batteries for high-end electronic devices and EV/HEV, the aerospace industry, and advanced engineering are projected to keep fuelling the strong demand momentum in the short/medium and long-term. Our consumption forecasts suggest that refined cobalt demand will register a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022, reaching 149,423 tonnes by 2022. Long-term consumption trends show that the Asian cobalt consumption share is expanding rapidly. Considering that Asian demand growth is mainly driven by chemical consumption, we believe that Asia, in particular China, is gaining the new cobalt market share established by cutting-edge technologies.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Executive Summary 5 CRU International Limited confidential Supply: Intermediates change cobalt raw material patterns The supply side of the cobalt market has recently been more complicated as a growing quantity of concentrate and intermediates has been produced and exported to feed refined production, mainly in China and Finland. CRU estimates that mine production amounted to 97,003 tonnes cobalt contained in 2011, 60% of which originated from the African Copper Belt. A total of 14 identified operations in the DRC produced ore and concentrate, most of which was exported as feed. Production of intermediate products totalled 35,456 tonnes in 2011; we expect that production of cobalt intermediates will reach 69,230 tonnes by 2017, replacing concentrate as feed in refined cobalt production. Major nickel and copper operations that do not produce refined cobalt metal will contribute to that growth. Intermediates will be the raw material of choice for refined producers due to higher cobalt content than concentrates, as well as logistical and processing efficiency. Total refined supply reached 79,796 tonnes in 2011, twice as high the level in 2000.

chunk.text: n concentrates, as well as logistical and processing efficiency. Total refined supply reached 79,796 tonnes in 2011, twice as high the level in 2000. Chemical products totalled 45,476 tonnes in 2011, primarily using cobalt units from the DRC. Production of refined chemicals is dominated by OMG, Chinese processors and the Yabulu refinery. We believe that small Chinese processors are cutting production in 2012, impacted by low prices and a demand slowdown. Looking ahead, it is anticipated that large-scale refiners will capture that loss, benefiting from lower processing costs. CRU projects that refined supply will amount to 111,867 tonnes by 2017; secondary cobalt supply from recycling (Umicore) is expected to gain momentum. This year, we present our production cost estimates based on the specific costs of producing cobalt. Half of forecast cobalt production in 2017 will be produced at around US$3.0/lb or below. This will come from nickel refineries and integrated metal producers. A considerable refined volume will be produced in the form of hydrometallurgical intermediates, at a cost of US$5.0-8.0/lb.

chunk.text: 6 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Executive Summary CRU International Limited confidential Upside risks to the price outlook Stronger than anticipated industrial and economic outlook. A stronger than expected industrial production activity will favour cobalt consumption. That will support several end-uses that consume superalloys. However, given the current economic outlook, the risks are mainly on the downside. A stronger than expected world economic activity will accelerate urbanisation trends, thus boosting the markets for high-end electronics and air travel. Favourable end-use technology developments that require a higher cobalt intensity of use. Examples include the emergence of tablets or a potential new end-use sector consuming rechargeable batteries; interestingly, robust growth of mobile phones and laptops significantly boosted cobalt consumption over the last 10 years. Delays in the start-up or ramp-up of new cobalt metal projects. New projects are always subject to operational and financial setbacks, even shortly before commissioning. Especially the projects located in higher risk regions may be subject to longer delays.

relations.evidence: consumption of superalloys, rechargeable batteries, mobile phones and laptops

chunk.text: al and financial setbacks, even shortly before commissioning. Especially the projects located in higher risk regions may be subject to longer delays. Despite the fact that CRU has been conservative in our forecasts, delays are always possible. Technical challenges at HPAL operations. There has been evidence that major HPAL operations are successfully commissioning. However, this kind of operations has historically experienced technical challenges that should not be disregarded when conducting a high and low case scenario analysis. Downside risks to the price outlook Weaker than anticipated industrial production activity. Considering the advanced level of downside macroeconomic risks, especially in industrialised countries, deterioration in industrial activity will have an impact on cobalt consumption. For instance, a weaker than expected world industrial activity will affect the market for cemented carbides as demand from the automotive, steel and shipbuilding industries will soften. Unfavourable technology developments that trigger substitution of cobalt or lower intensity of use. Miniaturisation trends in end-use markets will affect cobalt consumption.

relations.evidence: HPAL operations are successfully commissioning with evidence of technical challenges.

chunk.text: developments that trigger substitution of cobalt or lower intensity of use. Miniaturisation trends in end-use markets will affect cobalt consumption. This is very important in high-end devices that need to be lighter and smaller over time. The most critical end-use sector is lithium ion rechargeable batteries. Refined cobalt production stronger compared to CRU’s expectations. CRU has taken a conservative approach to forecasting refined cobalt production. If the actual production level is higher than what we project, the refined cobalt market will exhibit a higher surplus. Our conservative forecasting approach applies both to large and small scale operations. Aggressive development of new projects. If that takes place it will affect mainly the long-run outlook, considering the time needed to develop new projects. Lately, in view of the growing consumption volume of cobalt, as well as its contribution to high-end technologies, there has been a growing interest from investment groups to be involved in the cobalt industry.

relations.evidence: refined cobalt production stronger compared to CRU’s expectations

The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 CRU International Limited confidential Chapter 1 Cobalt demand Overview  Cobalt consumption continued to grow strongly in 2011, registering a 5.9% year-on-year increase to 77,015 tonnes. Demand from chemical sectors reached 44,150 tonnes, a 6.3% increase from the previous year. That was primarily driven by cobalt oxide used in lithium ion battery cells. Cobalt consumed by metallurgical sectors rose by 5.3% to 32,865 tonnes. The most important end-use sectors were cemented carbides and superalloys, particularly for aircraft engines and other engineering uses.  We predict that cobalt consumption will increase by 5.9% year-on-year to 81,554 tonnes in 2012. Despite a bearish sentiment in the global economy, cobalt consumption will benefit from robust end-use sectors. CRU projects that high-end markets, such as batteries for electronic devices and EV/HEV, the aerospace industry, and advanced engineering, will support long-run demand growth. It is expected that the world cobalt market will total 149,423 tonnes in 2022. This implies a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022.  Long-term trends show that the Asian cobalt consumption share is growing.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 1 14 CRU International Limited confidential 1. Uses of cobalt Cobalt is a ferromagnetic, silver white, hard, lustrous element. Its physical properties are similar to the ones of iron and nickel. Like iron, it can be magnetised. Cobalt is active chemically and can form many compounds. It is stable in air and unaffected by water; however, it can be slowly attacked by dilute acids. Cobalt-based blue pigments have been used since ancient times for jewellery and paints, and to create a distinctive blue tint to glass. CRU’s cobalt demand analysis is based on a real consumption methodology. Our demand estimates are expressed on a cobalt contained basis. We have identified 33 end-use sectors for cobalt, as presented in Table 1.1. For each end-use sector we analyse its market size and then take into consideration its cobalt-specific intensity of use to estimate real cobalt consumption. By following this methodology we avoid the potential problem due to changes in inventory levels that an apparent consumption based methodology would generate.

chunk.text: ing this methodology we avoid the potential problem due to changes in inventory levels that an apparent consumption based methodology would generate. In fact, an apparent consumption methodology in the case of cobalt would be questionable due to the complexity of trade flows as well as misclassifications in trade codes. A high-level segmentation of refined cobalt consumption is between the chemical and the metallurgical sectors. In the chemical sector, we conduct analysis on the following cobalt products: acetate, carbonate, chloride, hydroxide, oxide, nitrate, sulphate, and organics (carboxylates). The most important by market size is the lithium cobalt oxide, which is used in lithium ion battery cathodes. The material is composed of cobalt oxide layers in which the lithium is intercalated. During discharging, the lithium intercalated between the layers is set free as lithium ion. This is widely used in the batteries of electronic devices. In the metallurgical sector, the distinct properties of cobalt fulfil the requirements of a number of end-use sectors where strength, high ware resistance and high temperature resistance are demanded.

relations.evidence: The material is composed of cobalt oxide layers in which the lithium is intercalated.

chunk.text: s of cobalt fulfil the requirements of a number of end-use sectors where strength, high ware resistance and high temperature resistance are demanded. The cemented carbide sector is an important one for cobalt. Cemented carbides are composed of a metal matrix composite where carbide particles act as the aggregate and a metallic binder serves as the matrix. This solution is used in machining tough materials such as carbon steel or stainless steel, as well as in situations where other tools would ware. Additionally, the aerospace industry is an important market for cobalt. Indeed, performance in hot corrosion and resistance, along with reasonable weldability, means that cobalt alloys are favoured in various rotating parts for jet engines, particularly fan blades. As analysed in this chapter, cobalt is consumed by high-end markets. Despite cobalt’s use by cutting edge industries, final consumers are not aware of its contribution to economic and industrial growth. Rarely does a final consumer know that cobalt is an essential element in the battery of smartphones, prosthetics or aircraft engines.

The European Commission has identified cobalt as one of the 14 economically important raw materials. From a demand point of view, cobalt contributes to economic growth of developing regions. The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 1 15 CRU International Limited confidential 2. Demand for cobalt in 2011 This section refers to Table 1.1. We estimate that world refined cobalt consumption amounted to 77,015 tonnes in 2011. The figure represented a 5.9% year-on-year increase from the previous year. This rate was a deceleration from 13.9% growth registered in 2010; this is attributed first to a return to more sustainable demand growth rates after the rebound from the global financial crisis, and second to a slowdown in the global recovery in 2011 attributed to a number of macroeconomic and other factors. CRU believes that cobalt demand growth is healthy and comfortably outperforms advances in the global industrial production (IP) index, which captures a broad range of economic activity. In 2011, world IP rose by 4.8%; the fact that cobalt consumption growth outperformed the general IP index is backed by cobalt’s contribution to growing end-use markets, which are less vulnerable to the current macroeconomic downside risks. Looking at long-term consumption trends, refined cobalt demand in 2011 was up by 90% when compared to 2000 levels.

relations.evidence: world refined cobalt consumption amounted to 77,015 tonnes in 2011

chunk.text: rent macroeconomic downside risks. Looking at long-term consumption trends, refined cobalt demand in 2011 was up by 90% when compared to 2000 levels. Refined cobalt consumption grew at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 6.0% in 2000-2011, which includes a modest contraction of 0.5% year-on-year in 2009. 2.1 Cobalt use in chemical sectors Refined cobalt consumption in chemical forms reached 44,150 tonnes in 2011, a 6.3% increase from 2010. Demand for cobalt in chemical forms made 57% of total refined consumption in 2011. Chemical cobalt consumption advanced at a CAGR of 8.2% in 2000-2011, contributing significantly to the expansion of the total cobalt market. Cobalt oxide used in lithium ion battery cells for electronic devices accounted for 53% of chemical consumption in 2011. This particular end-use accounted for 30% of total (chemical and metallurgical) refined cobalt consumption in 2011. Cobalt oxide in lithium ion batteries registered a 16.3% CAGR in 2000-2011, by far the strongest of any other large-sized end-use sector. The most important final products we have taken into consideration in our analysis are mobile phones and laptops.

chunk.text: y other large-sized end-use sector. The most important final products we have taken into consideration in our analysis are mobile phones and laptops. The world market for mobile phones amounted to 1.47 billion units in 2011; 32% of this figure was smartphones while 68% standard (non-smartphone) mobile phones. CRU believes that cobalt intensity of use in smartphones amounted to an average of 5.9 grams per unit, while that of standard mobile phones 3.5 grams per unit. When estimating consumption in the long-run we incorporate the increasing market share of smartphones as well as the effects of final product miniaturisation. Demand for cobalt acetate rose by 6.1% year-on-year to 4,474 tonnes in 2011. This is an important market for cobalt, which expanded at a CAGR of 6.8% in 2000-2011. The principal end-use of cobalt acetate is the manufacture of PET, which is mainly used to produce polyester fibres for synthetic textiles, packaging, and bottles. Clean plastic bottles in particular have seen strong growth, supported by the increase in the sales of bottled water and soft/energy drinks. Cobalt acetate is used as a catalyst.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 1 16 CRU International Limited confidential Historically, cobalt oxide used in the manufacture of ceramic colourings to make ‘cobalt blue’ was an important end-use sector for cobalt. This end-use saw a contraction in the 1990s; however, this market experienced a rebirth further to its migration to Asia. Consumption totalled 4,306 tonnes in 2011, up by 7.6% from 2010. Supported by the Asia-based revival, cobalt oxide consumption in ceramics advanced at a CAGR of 10.7% in 2000-2011. CRU introduced a new end-use sector in our cobalt demand analysis. This is cobalt sulphate consumed in copper electro wining (EW) production. The EW operations we have considered in our model jointly amounted to 3.24 million tonnes of refined copper and accounted for 93% of world EW production in 2011. These operations are located in Chile, the USA, the DRC, Zambia, Peru, Mexico, and China. Cobalt intensity of use in each country varies within the range of 103-154 grams of cobalt contained in sulphate per tonne of EW copper produced. 2.2 Cobalt use in metallurgical sectors Refined cobalt consumed by metallurgical sectors totalled 32,865 tonnes in 2011.

The figure increased by 5.3% from the previous year and 51% from 2000. Demand from metallurgical sectors rose at a CAGR of 3.8% in 2000-2011. The demand growth in metallurgical sectors was slower compared to that in chemical sectors. We estimate that cemented carbides made 6,652 tonnes of refined cobalt consumption in 2011. Cobalt consumption by this end-use sector rose by 5.0% from the previous year. It is a growing market, having seen a CAGR of 6.3% in 2000-2011. Sumitomo Electric Industries commented that demand in this market has been increasing on a global scale due to economic development and growing automobile production especially in emerging regions. Cemented carbides are essential for machining all kinds of mechanical parts in the automotive, steel, shipbuilding and IT industries. Moreover, Sandvik said that in 2011 the global market for metal-cutting tools as well as wear parts and components in cemented carbide and other hard materials was estimated to total 165 billion SEK.

Sandvik estimated that the average annual growth rate in this segment has been 4-5%. Interestingly, this is consistent with our view, which suggests that cobalt demand from the cemented carbide and diamond tool markets advanced at a CAGR of 4.5% in 2000-2011. Demand for superalloys used in aircraft engine rotating parts saw a 7.3% year-on-year growth to 4,545 tonnes in 2011. The civil aerospace business of Rolls-Royce delivered 962 engines in 2011, an increase of 13.7% year-on-year. The defence aerospace business of the same engine manufacturer reported a 14.6% year-on-year increase to 814 deliveries in 2011. In addition, Boeing said that it expects to deliver 585-600 commercial aircraft in 2012, an increase of 22.6- 25.8% year-on-year. Considering the time lag between cobalt use in engine building and final aircraft delivery, the above means that cobalt consumption growth by this end-use sector was robust in 2011. Cobalt in superalloys used in other engineering sectors reached an estimated 5,215 tonnes in 2011.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 1 20 CRU International Limited confidential 3. Demand for cobalt by region This section refers to Table 1.2. The long term regional consumption development is central to the analysis of the cobalt market. Looking at changes in 2000-2011 allows us to derive concrete conclusions. This period has seen aggressive growth in the consumption of emerging economies, production migration of end-use sectors, advances in technology, socio-economic development on a global basis, as well as the global financial crisis. Therefore, it would be interesting to study these developments from a cobalt consumption standpoint. As in the analysis of every commodity, the most striking development is the emergence of China as the largest cobalt consumer in the world. Refined cobalt consumption in China rose from 3,780 tonnes in 2000 to 27,053 tonnes in 2011. This represents a CAGR of 19.6%, the fastest of any other region or country. In terms of world percentage share, Chinese cobalt consumption surged from 9.3% in 2000 to 35.1% in 2011. This has mainly been triggered by soaring cobalt oxide consumption by domestic lithium ion battery makers.

relations.evidence: soaring cobalt oxide consumption by domestic lithium ion battery makers

chunk.text: surged from 9.3% in 2000 to 35.1% in 2011. This has mainly been triggered by soaring cobalt oxide consumption by domestic lithium ion battery makers. The manufacture of batteries has traditionally been an Asian business, which used to take place in the past primarily in North East Asia. That used to serve the local electronics supply chains. Therefore, as these supply chains migrated to China, battery production moved there too. Despite robust growth in the Chinese share of refined cobalt consumption, this is still less than other major industrial metals that CRU covers. For instance, the Chinese share of refined copper consumption in 2011was 39.6%, while that of primary aluminium 43.1%. This is attributed to the fact that despite the strong growth by chemical sectors in China, the metallurgical sectors have not registered a similar one. Indeed, the Chinese consumption share of world metallurgical demand rose from 10.7% in 2000 to 23.6% in 2011. This is because China has not benefited yet from the strong cobalt demand growth by the aerospace industry and advanced engineering.

We assume that commercial aircraft engine manufacturing in China will commence in 2016, therefore cobalt consumption is anticipated to start a year earlier. The Rest of Asia saw the second fastest demand growth between 2000 and 2011. Cobalt consumption registered a CAGR of 5.0% to 21,137 tonnes in 2011. This region has exhibited a different growth pattern compared to China, exhibiting signs of a mature market. In our demand model, this region mainly consists of Japan, South Korea and Taiwan. On the one hand the Rest of Asia has benefited from strong demand from the lithium ion battery cell segment, on the other hand it has suffered due to production migration of this sector to China. In 2000, the USA was the largest cobalt consumer in the world with a share of 34.2%. Consumption marginally contracted from 13,825 tonnes in 2000 to 13,093 tonnes in 2011. However, the US demand share plunged from 34.2% to 17.0% in that period.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 1 24 CRU International Limited confidential 4. Demand for cobalt by end-use sector This section refers to Table 1.3. The reason that the chemical sector became so important in the cobalt market is the growth in lithium ion cell batteries. We estimate that this market amounted to 23,434 tonnes of cobalt in 2011. China accounted for 59% of that volume, followed by the Rest of Asia with 40% and the USA with 1%. While battery production mainly takes place in China, there is still significant production in North East Asia. Cobalt consumed by lithium ion batteries for electronic devices accounted for 30.4% of total world refined consumption. A growing segment for cobalt that has not seen its full potential yet is electric vehicles (EV) and hybrid electric vehicles (HEV). So far, EV/HEV have mainly been using nickel metal hydride and nickel cadmium batteries, which consume cobalt hydroxide. CRU estimates that cobalt hydroxide used by batteries in EV/HEV reached 1,824 tonnes in 2011. The Rest of Asia accounted for 73% of that volume which supplies final products, such as the Toyota Prius.

Even though lithium ion batteries have started supplying the EV/HEV segment, their share was still small, hence using only an estimated 332 tonnes on a world level in 2011. World demand for cobalt acetate, which is principally used in the production of PET, totalled 4,474 tonnes in 2011. Even though it is consumed in all regions, the Rest of Asia accounted for 41% of the world total cobalt acetate consumption. This is attributed to large PET production volumes especially in South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, India, and Indonesia. Despite significant imports of PET, the USA remains a major producer of this commodity. US acetate consumption was 990 tonnes in 2011. Indeed, demand for acetate accounted for 26.3% of total US cobalt chemical consumption in 2011. Cobalt acetate is consumed around the world due to the fact that production of PET is a global business. Demand from metallurgical sectors made 42.7% of total refined cobalt consumption in 2011. Out of total metallurgical demand, the USA and Western Europe accounted for 56.6%.

Cobalt in superalloys used in aircraft engines totalled 4,545 tonnes in 2011, which was almost equally distributed between the USA and Western Europe. We assume that, at the moment, commercial large-scale production of aircraft engines takes place only in the above regions. Cobalt consumption in cemented carbides and diamond tools reached 8,051 tonnes in 2011. Historically, this is one of the most important metallurgical sectors of cobalt. Its development originated in Germany in the 1920s; it was introduced in the USA shortly afterwards and subsequent development occurred in other industrialised countries. In 2011, the USA, China, the Rest of Asia, and Western Europe jointly accounted for 7,647 tonnes or 95% of cobalt used in carbides and diamond tools on a world level. Within the field of engineering materials, cemented carbides have a crucial role as they combine high hardness and strength with good toughness within a good property range. However, production requires advanced engineering.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 1 28 CRU International Limited confidential 5. Forecast demand for cobalt This section refers to Table 1.4. CRU projects that demand for refined cobalt will rise by 5.9% year-on-year to 81,554 tonnes in 2012. We believe that despite the prevailing macroeconomic challenges, robust growth of end-use sectors will continue to support cobalt consumption. Cobalt consumption by chemical end-use sectors is predicted to expand by 7.2% to 47,310 tonnes in 2012. That will correspond to 58% of total cobalt demand, up from 57% in 2011. Our aim at this stage is to analyse the changes in the projected consumption shares by region. The findings of this analysis complete the ones of the earlier section which focuses on the same changes, however in the historic period 2000-2011. Anticipated changes in regional consumption shares are based both on global socio-economic trends and cobalt-specific trends. To forecast changes in the downstream sectors of the cobalt market we take into consideration supply chain trends of end-use sectors, production migration, transfer of technology from industrialised to industrialising regions, as well as other factors. We project that Chinese refined cobalt consumption will continue to increase strongly, however, at a slower pace. Demand for cobalt in China is forecast to grow by 8.6% year-on-year to 29,375 tonnes in 2012. Cobalt consumption is anticipated to register a CAGR of 9.1% in 2012- 2017 and 7.3% in 2017-2022.

chunk.text: 38 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential 1. Cobalt reserve base Cobalt is usually found in low concentrations and is extracted primarily as a by- or co-product of mining for other metals, mainly nickel and copper. The Cobalt Development Institute estimated that in 2011 55% of cobalt production came from nickel mining, 35% from the mining of copper and other minerals and 10% from primary cobalt sources. Cobalt is widely scattered in the earth‟s crust and a concentration can be found in potentially exploitable quantities in several countries in the world. Significant resources of cobalt are also present in the deep-sea nodules and crusts which occur in the Mid-Pacific, although these are not economically viable with current technology and economic conditions. According to the United States Geological Survey (USGS), as much as 1 billion tons of hypothetical and speculative cobalt resources may exist in manganese nodules and crusts on the ocean floor. Economic concentrations of cobalt can be found in three different geological settings.

relations.evidence: Cobalt is extracted primarily as a by- or co-product of mining for other metals, mainly nickel and copper.

chunk.text: may exist in manganese nodules and crusts on the ocean floor. Economic concentrations of cobalt can be found in three different geological settings. The first is in sediment hosted, which can be found in the African Copper Belt, the world‟s most important cobalt source. The second is hydrothermal and volcanogenic, for example at Bou Azzer in Morocco. The third geological setting is magmatic sulphide and related laterite deposits, which can be found in Cuba, New Caledonia, Australia and Russia. 1.1 Conventional assessments Through the years, geologists, mining engineers and other specialists operating in the minerals field have used various terms to describe and classify cobalt mineral reserves and resources. Some of these terms have gained wide use and acceptance, although they are not always used with precisely the same meaning. We use the definition of resources and reserves by the USGS which states that a cobalt resource is a concentration of naturally occurring solid material most often within the Earth‟s crust in such form and amount that economic extraction of a commodity from the concentration can be potentially feasible.

chunk.text: st often within the Earth‟s crust in such form and amount that economic extraction of a commodity from the concentration can be potentially feasible. The resource should meet specified minimum physical and chemical criteria in order to be mined viably in regards to grade, quality, thickness and depth. The reserve, however, is considered to be a demonstrated (measured plus indicated) part of the resource which could be economically extracted or produced at the time of determination. Cobalt sources have changed over history, from Norway, Sweden, Hungary and Germany to the dependence on the African Copper Belt. In the last decade, sources have diversified with increasing amounts coming from nickel and copper ores as well as recycled scrap and cobalt containing intermediates. The USGS estimated that the physically available resource and reserve base for cobalt worldwide was 15.2 million tonnes and 7.7 million tonnes respectively in 2011.

As an example, the Zambian Synclinorium project by Mopani, which in turn is largely owned by Glencore, has measured, indicated and inferred cobalt resources of 320,000 tonnes. Extensive exploration is also taking place in Cameroon by GeoCam. Geovic owns 60.5% of Geovic Cameroon, while the National Investment Corporation of Cameroon, controls 39.5%. Geovic‟s mine permit covers 1,250 square km and provides exclusive production rights to seven large cobalt, nickel and manganese deposits. Two of the seven deposits, Nkamouna and Mada (directly adjacent to Nkamouna), are the first planned for mining and production. Geovic Cameroon has identified the world‟s largest known primary cobalt deposit, with significant nickel and manganese credits.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 43 CRU International Limited confidential 2. Mine production After cobalt resources and reserves, the next stage in the production of refined cobalt is mine production. In detail, cobalt production is dependent on ore and concentrates from many sources, some of which are relatively obscure. There are several small nickel mines for example in Australia that produce nickel concentrates that contain cobalt which are shipped overseas for refining in Canada, Finland and China. Similarly, operations in Botswana, Brazil and Canada send mainly nickel concentrates containing cobalt to major smelting and refining operations in their respective countries. We have therefore listed the operations where cobalt is mined in Table 2.3 overleaf. We have identified 51 operations where cobalt ore is mined and show where the respective ores and concentrates are processed. The objective in our tracking of cobalt production at the mine level has been to provide a check on our refined production numbers and particularly to reconcile the volumes of material imported by China and Finland where major volumes of refined metal and chemicals are produced.

chunk.text: and particularly to reconcile the volumes of material imported by China and Finland where major volumes of refined metal and chemicals are produced. As identified in Table 2.3, mined production was nearly 73,000 tonnes of contained cobalt in 2009. This increased by nearly 25% year-on-year to just over 90,000 tonnes in 2010 and a further 8% to 97,000 tonnes in 2011. The largest source of mined cobalt production over this period has been the African Copper Belt, with the DRC and Zambia supplying over 60% of this material (59,000 tonnes of cobalt contained). We have identified 14 operations in the DRC, which collectively produced 53,300 tonnes of cobalt in ores and concentrates in 2011. Operations in Russia, Cuba, Australia and Canada contributed a further 19,000 tonnes of contained cobalt during the period while the lateritic deposits in the Pacific found in Indonesia, New Caledonia and the Philippines supplied the bulk of the remainder. The operations in the African Copper Belt are extremely significant as they are probably going to be the source of choice for increasing supply in the longer term, particularly to China.

The 14 operations currently producing ores and concentrates in the DRC reflect the abundance of deposits and the relative ease in establishing a mine with a heavy media separating plant that can produce concentrates. Some of these operations such as Kov, Kamoto and Tenke Fungurume are large scale integrated mine and metallurgical facilities but nevertheless, it is not inconceivable that future mining operations in the DRC could support production of a further 30-40,000 tonnes of cobalt in the long-term. One other feature that should not be overlooked is that mine production volumes are around 20% higher than refined output. This accounts for the loss in recovery of cobalt units in the refining of both metal and chemicals.

chunk.text: 48 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential 3. The impact of cobalt raw material flows on refined production A great deal of refined cobalt production has come to rely on concentrates, hydrometallurgical intermediates and alliage blanc, a white alloy of copper, cobalt and iron. Virtually all of the refined cobalt in China, both metal and chemicals, is based on imported concentrates and intermediates. A similar situation with refined chemicals exists in Finland. Since 2000, the production and trade in cobalt concentrates and cobalt intermediates has grown ten-fold. Taking into account that in the processing of these concentrates and intermediates, there is a loss in recovery, we estimate that in 2000, the useable cobalt in concentrates amounted to just over 4,000 tonnes. By 2011, this figure reached over 44,000 tonnes boosted by a growing trade in cobalt intermediates, as indicated in Table 2.4.

In our assessment of the volumes of this production and trade, we have assumed that the useable cobalt in refining operations represents, 75% of cobalt contained in concentrates, 80% of alliage blanc and 85% of hydrometallurgical intermediates such as crude cobalt carbonate, hydroxide and mixed nickel-cobalt sulphides. In 2011, China accounted for nearly 33,000 tonnes of useable cobalt contained in these raw materials and Finland, nearly 12,000 tonnes. Over the past few years, there has been some concern about the apparent disconnect between the volumes of concentrates and intermediates being produced and imported into China.

Various observers have estimated that this surplus inventory could exceed 25,000 tonnes of contained cobalt. In detail, when the volumes are examined on a useable cobalt basis as shown in Table 2.4, we calculate that over the period 2000 to 2011, China imported 190,000 tonnes of useable cobalt in raw materials and produced 173,000 tonnes of metal and refined chemicals over the same period. We therefore estimate that this surplus inventory, in tonnes of useable cobalt, is in fact around 17,000 tonnes at present. In the case of Finland, OMG the producer of specialty cobalt chemicals, has increasingly relied on these materials for its production. In the early part of this period, the company sourced a great deal of material from Russia and then moved to importing concentrates. Recently, we see from trade statistics that most of its raw materials are now sourced in the form of hydrometallurgical intermediates from the DRC. chunk.text: 50 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential cobalt intermediates will account for around 12,000 tonnes and crude cobalt carbonate, some 9,000 tonnes by the end of this forecast period. With this significant expansion in the production and trade of intermediates the concern has been whether a significant surplus of raw materials will be created by 2017 and beyond. Will there be sufficient need and will there be sufficient refining capacity? Overall, we believe that as these hydrometallurgical intermediates are of higher cobalt content than concentrates and they are easier and more efficient to process, these materials will be the first choice of refined metal and chemical producers. In China, we are seeing a rapid increase in the consumption of intermediates and a decline in the use of concentrates (Table 2.4) as well as alliage blanc. Therefore, we are not expecting a surplus of intermediates to be produced, but we will probably see a decline in the use of concentrates out to 2017. In the longer term, many companies that are currently using imported concentrates and intermediates are forecasting expansions to their operations.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 51 CRU International Limited confidential In Table 2.6, we identify the various refined metal and chemicals producers that use traded concentrates and intermediates and show their forecast production out to 2017 and in 2022. We have also aggregated forecast cobalt containing intermediates, concentrates and lateritic ore availability in useable cobalt units. Out to 2017, we are of the opinion that there will be more than enough potential supply of these materials to meet forecast production. As the production of intermediates increases, we expect the need for concentrates to fall. In fact, our concentrate use forecasts have been calculated as a balancing figure. However in the long-term, that is by 2022, we see that the picture could change quite dramatically. We are showing a small surplus of material on the basis of forecast intermediates supply and a nominal 7,700 tonnes of cobalt in concentrates from the DRC. However this could very easily disappear. Firstly, demand in China for metal and chemicals is expected to be some 25,000 tonnes greater than forecast production by the companies indentified in Table 2.6.

The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 55 CRU International Limited confidential 5. Cobalt refined production in 2011 In spite of well anticipated and often substantial production cuts at leading refined producers around the world last year, such as Katanga Mining in the DRC, primary cobalt production rose again emphatically overall to 78,296 tonnes, gaining 9.5%. In 2011, output of cobalt specialty products and chemicals rose most strongly; by 11.8% to 45,476 tonnes. This segment of the market accounted for a 58% share of total primary output, up from 57% in 2010. Interestingly, China‟s production share of specialty products fell last year, largely because of a large decline at leading operations Zhejiang Huayou and Jinchuan, both of whom who had been unwilling, given slackening market fundamentals, to maintain full capacity as in 2010. Indeed Huayou, a 6,000 tpy chemicals producer by capacity, produced 4,000 tonnes last year, down from 5,800 tonnes in 2010; a level supported by an excess of raw material the company had been keen to draw down.

relations.evidence: Cobalt refined production in 2011

chunk.text: oduced 4,000 tonnes last year, down from 5,800 tonnes in 2010; a level supported by an excess of raw material the company had been keen to draw down. Overall, however, specialty production in China still rose impressively to 29,839 tonnes last year or 66% of the global total, supported by Huayou‟s neighbour Galico, Ganzhou Hengsheng Umicore and some smaller facilities. The largest growth, however, came from outside of China, for example India, albeit from a low starting point, but also the Yabulu refinery in Australia, which had another strong growth year in reaching 2,631 tonnes up from 2,189 tonnes in 2010. On the metal producing side, Katanga Mining – the third largest metal producer in 2010 with the reported potential to become number one – was not alone in recording a decline, though the 29.2% retreat to just 2,433 tonnes was the largest of all major producers. While the company continued to be impacted by an oversupplied market, lower cobalt head grades and electricity constraints were major factors in last year‟s decline. Electricity constraints affected various operations last year, particularly in the final quarter.

chunk.text: 58 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential 6. Cobalt refined production forecast Although further erosion in cobalt supply can be expected from various producers this year again, including Katanga Mining, who continued to be affected by power disruptions in the DRC during the first quarter, we expect another year of strong albeit slowing growth in refined production. Unlike the past few years, however, when specialty, chemical grade supply growth has driven the refined market, we still believe metal will become the driver, albeit partly to fill a gap in specialty supply caused by the downstream production plans underway in the DRC. In fact this year we forecast a decline in chemical cobalt production of 1.0%, before a relatively modest 3.8% per year rise is forecast out to 2017. CRU predicts that small Chinese chemical producers will cut production in 2012, affected by low market prices. Like last year, however, there will be a mix of developments from one producer to another, particularly within China.

Contrary to last year‟s trend, we expect supply from Gangzhou Hengsheng Umicore will fall, albeit modestly, while the weakening pricing environment – and metal expansion at Gecamines, DRC – will disrupt some of the country‟s smaller players. However, this vacuum in supply will provide opportunities for the larger-scale producers including Jinchuan and Huayou Cobalt who reported production cuts last year. Nevertheless, as far as the chemical market is concerned, we believe market leader OMG is well placed to increase its share of the global market. Having produced over 10,400 tonnes of refined cobalt last year, the Cleveland-based conglomerate is forecast to surpass 11,000 tonnes this year and remain the largest producer through the forecast period.

Clearly, profitability has been affected over the past year by a weakening cobalt price – their adjusted operating profit slipped to US$81m in the main cobalt division, Advanced Materials in 2011 – but their outlook remains robust, supported particularly by demand for tablets and smartphones. During the next five years we forecast the company‟s growth will exceed the rest of the specialty sector. The CAGR from 2012 to 2017 is predicted to be 6.0%, compared to 3.1% for other specialty producers. Though impressive, OMG‟s potential pales next to metal producers who as a combined group will surpass the specialty industry by 2015 and represent 52% of the refined market by 2017. In addition to the growth in primary production, CRU believes that over the forecast period secondary production will gather momentum. We predict that environmental and cost pressures will lead to a rise in the recycling of batteries. As cobalt is a valuable metal, an increase in recycling volumes will minimise losses. chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 61 CRU International Limited confidential 7. Costs of production In our outlook, we have adopted a different approach to estimating the costs of producing cobalt. Instead of using our value-based costing method, which allocated cobalt production costs in accordance with the value of cobalt and the main metal, we have attempted to estimate the specific costs of cobalt production at the key operations. We have made this change because it is essential to understand how the cobalt supply chain is going to respond to the rise in processing of cobalt concentrates and intermediates in China and the rapid increase in the amounts of cobalt intermediates, such as crude cobalt hydroxide or carbonate that are being produced at current copper and nickel operations and projects. The supply chain has become elongated and in detail, more complex. Table 2.10 below shows our grouping of the different types of cobalt production and how the volumes of production are expected to evolve by 2017.

relations.evidence: production of cobalt

chunk.text: ex. Table 2.10 below shows our grouping of the different types of cobalt production and how the volumes of production are expected to evolve by 2017. Currently, the Chinese operations, which in the main produce refined cobalt chemicals from purchased concentrates and intermediates, have the highest cost base and as such, they represent the “marginal cost” section of the supply cost curve. Some of the recent transaction prices charged for concentrates and intermediates may not be sustainable in the long term. By contrast, around 60% of all refined cobalt, mainly metal, is produced as a by-product in the late stages of copper and nickel refining at nickel refineries and integrated metal operations. The specific cost of production of cobalt in these operations, particularly at the nickel refineries, is therefore very much lower as the main metal assumes most of the cost of mining and processing. In addition to these three groups of producers are the main product cobalt producers and, as mentioned earlier, the intermediates producers that produce crude cobalt hydroxide, sulphide and carbonate in hydrometallurgical operations and sell this to third parties.

The economics of cobalt supply have therefore become more dependent on the transfer pricing of concentrates and intermediates and their refining costs. We therefore believe that the most appropriate approach to establishing the current supply curve is to estimate the specific costs of producing cobalt at the key operations and delivering it to the market, as it is these costs that will determine the future behaviour of producers of refined cobalt. We stress at this point that our figures must be regarded as estimates as we have at times been forced to use some typical industry operating parameters at some operations. However, in defence of our approach, we believe we have been reasonably consistent in our assessments and by modelling the costs of each operation within these five different groups and this has enabled us to identify some of the critical cost points for each group.

chunk.text: g the costs of each operation within these five different groups and this has enabled us to identify some of the critical cost points for each group. This section of the report therefore begins with a short description of our methodology and source materials. We then describe the key features of the 2011/12 cost curve and explain how we expect this will evolve by 2017. 7.1 Estimates of specific cobalt production costs Our estimates of cobalt production costs for by-product cobalt have come from the CRU in- house copper and nickel production cost models. Virtually all of by-product cobalt is produced in the nickel refineries and the integrated metal producers and collectively, this represents the largest volume of refined cobalt production. We have focused exclusively on the specific costs of producing cobalt at these operations. Generally speaking this has involved assessing the costs in the purification of the cobalt leach liquor and the electro-winning of cobalt metal or the case of the Sherritt process, the hydrometallurgical precipitation, sintering and briquetting of the metal.

chunk.text: he electro-winning of cobalt metal or the case of the Sherritt process, the hydrometallurgical precipitation, sintering and briquetting of the metal. Our cost estimates have been prepared from a bottom-up approach, assessing the costs of power, labour and consumables for the purification and electro-winning or briquetting stages. We have also estimated the costs of delivering the cobalt produced into the key markets of the Western Hemisphere and China where appropriate. Where we have relevant information, we have included the costs of maintenance, sustaining and working capital. The operations where we have included these costs are generally the integrated metal producers where we have assembled detailed cost profiles in the CRU copper and nickel cost models. In the nickel refineries, where cobalt is produced at a very late stage in the refining process, we have assumed that these costs would be absorbed by the main metal, nickel. For operations where cobalt is the main product, we have taken typical, mining, milling, smelting and refining costs for the operations involved.

The CRU nickel cost model also contains profiles of some main product cobalt operations. In our assessment of the Chinese processing operations, we have looked at the three types of cobalt raw materials imported into China at present: alliage blanc, cobalt concentrates and hydro metallurgical intermediates. We have used our estimated cobalt contents of these materials, recovery rates and the current pricing practices for these materials. For example, we have assumed that in the case of cobalt concentrates, they contain 6% cobalt, recovery is 75% and payment is made at 60% of the metal price. For hydrometallurgical intermediates such as crude carbonate and hydroxide, cobalt content is much higher at 25%, recovery is also better at 85% and price paid is 70% of the metal price.

We have also estimated overland freight costs out of the DRC, ocean freight and internal Chinese freight charges in order to assess the full costs of these materials before they are leached and converted to chemicals. In assessing the costs of delivering refined cobalt to market, we have continued to use the “realisation cost” estimates from our cost models. Realisation costs include freight charges from the production centre into a major market, marketing and sales costs and interest on the cost of the pipeline that is required to be established for delivery. Where a crude intermediate is produced, our approach assumes that either a refining cost or a refining charge will need to be estimated in order to fully quantify the realisations costs. In the case of the OMG Big Hill operation, alliage blanc is sent to the company‟s operations in Finland for further processing. In this case we assume the material attracts a refining cost as part of the realisations costs.

However, in the case of the crude hydroxide produced by Tenke Fungurume, we assume that this incurs a refining charge as this is effectively sold to a third party. The high cost figure represents the cost of processing purchased materials and supplying a local Chinese market. We have seen that the lowest cost operations using purchased materials are the ones processing hydrometallurgical intermediates. Concentrate processing operations are the most expensive. One other feature of note is that the cost of processing refined cobalt is higher than metal price quotations such as the MB or LME by about $4/lb and this represented 25,000 tonnes of refined cobalt production in 2011. The lowest cost estimate of producing refined cobalt is at the Jinchuan operations. This is a result of the fact that cobalt refining takes place at the late stage of nickel and copper refining and that the main metals bears virtually all the cost until the cobalt feed is purified and electro- winning takes place. Jinchuan has lower labour and consumables costs than the other nickel refineries which give it a small cost advantage.

The key part of this analysis is that the combined production of both the nickel refineries and the integrated metal producers have an effective cost of production and sales of cobalt of below $3/lb. These two groups of producers represented 31,000 tonnes of production in 2011. In the middle ground, are the three main product cobalt producers: Kasese, Chambishi Slag Processing (Cosac) and CTT which have costs of $7.40/lb; $14.38/lb and $9.11/lb respectively. The Chambishi slag operation suffers from having to process a much lower grade of slag than the OMG Big Hill operation and a very much higher power tariff, which for a pyrometallurgical operation is most significant. Currently, this operation can only be marginally profitable. The other important feature is the costs of producing intermediates. The Ruashi operation is currently producing cobalt hydroxide which is being shipped for processing at Jinchuan‟s refinery. As such it has very low site costs but incurs a refining cost as part of the operation‟s realisation costs.

Effective production and sales costs are $3.56/lb but the site costs, which represent the cost of producing the carbonate are only $0.39/lb. From a mining and processing viewpoint, these costs are low and from Jinchuan‟s perspective, this is a very low cost operation in terms of producing refined cobalt in China. It does appear that the production of hydrometallurgical intermediates for refining in China is currently very profitable and currently represents around 19,000 tonnes of refined cobalt. The Tenke Fungurume cost estimate which includes a tolling charge, has total costs of around $6.42/lb. The full cost in China however when using purchased material is almost three times that figure. There are therefore obvious advantages for Chinese processors and other companies with processing capability in taking equity stakes or even full ownership in DRC operations producing intermediates. The OMG Big Hill operation is also testimony to this fact, even though the pyrometallurgical route is more expensive.

chunk.text: 66 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 2 CRU International Limited confidential The 2011/12 cobalt cost curve is therefore characterised by a large zone of costs below $3/lb and another slightly smaller zone of very high costs of around $18-22/lb. In between, there is a small zone of main product producers with costs between $7-14/lb and then a zone of around 19,000 tonnes of production with costs in the range of $4-6.50/lb. There is no doubt that the current marginal section of the cost curve is predicated on purchased raw materials and one which favours the hydrometallurgical intermediates. We have also seen that when a Chinese or other company such as OMG, takes an equity stake in an intermediate operation, their production costs fall significantly. We therefore expect to see some significant changes in the shape of the cost curve over the next five years. 7.3 Forecast cobalt costs 2017 Our detailed cost estimates of nominal cobalt production costs in 2017 are provided in Table 2.12 towards the end of this section.

chunk.text: cobalt costs 2017 Our detailed cost estimates of nominal cobalt production costs in 2017 are provided in Table 2.12 towards the end of this section. As can be seen the 2017 cost curve overleaf, we estimate that effective production and sales costs of refined cobalt ranged from a low of $1.90/lb to a high of just under $21/lb with a forecast average price of cobalt of $14/lb in 2017. The key characteristics of this cost curve are very similar to the 2011/12 cost curve but the volume of production will be considerably higher as shown in Table 2.9. Refined production covered in this analysis is close to 100,000 tonnes. In addition to this we are expecting nearly 32,000 tonnes of cobalt contained in intermediates to be produced and a further 11,000 tonnes of cobalt contained in mixed nickel cobalt intermediates. We are expecting around 55,000 tonnes of cobalt, which is over half of all refined cobalt production, to be produced at nickel refineries and integrated metal producers which will have specific production and sales costs of between $2 -3.12/lb.

Cobalt price quotations 1.1 London Metal Exchange (LME) Since February 22nd 2010, cobalt has been traded on the LME, making prices – at least for lower grade 99.3% but high volume metal – more transparent compared to freemarket quotations. The contract specifications are as follows:  Minimum 99.3%  Cathodes (broken or cut), ingots, briquettes or rounds  1 tonne lots (100-500kg drums of uniform size and weight)  Quoted in US dollars per tonne  Delivered by brand The LME announced that cobalt trade on the exchange amounted to 6,578 tonnes in 2011. CRU estimates that this volume was equivalent to 8.5% of world refined cobalt consumption in 2011. In the first half of 2012, LME cobalt trade totalled 5,592 tonnes. If cobalt trade increases in the second half of 2012 at the same pace as in the first half, we anticipate that full year cobalt trade on the LME will reach 11,184 tonnes, up by 70% from year-ago levels. That would equate to 13.7% of world refined cobalt consumption. This development would potentially support the role of the LME as an emerging marketplace for cobalt trade.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 75 CRU International Limited confidential 1.2 Price assessments Metal Bulletin (MB) and Platts Metals Week publish regular prices for cobalt. Prices are obtained from telephone surveys with traders and consumers in Europe and North America. Since 1991, Metal Bulletin has been publishing the prices of two grades of metal: 99.8% metal (high grade) and 99.3% metal (low grade). These are currently the de facto benchmark prices for cobalt, though Platts Metals Week assessments specifically for the US market are still prevalent there. Also in the USA, the US Defence Logistics Agency‟s (DLA) Strategic Materials Directorate had been selling cobalt from its stockpile since 1993, which at the time amounted to 52m pounds, with the sales price published after each transaction. DLA sales historically played a role in the price discovery process, but as stocks have diminished are now much less relevant, with only a handful of small transactions concluded each year depending on business conditions. In the 2009 financial year, for example, only 412,320 lbs were sold for just over $6.8m. No sales have taken place since 2009.

After 2000, several other price reporting systems emerged. The Cobalt Open Sales System (COSS) was an internet trading platform originally developed by WMC Resources for the sales of its 99.8% cobalt cathode, produced at Falconbridge‟s Nikkelverk refinery. BHP Billiton continued this system after the takeover of WMC. In addition to BHP Billiton, other producers, notably Norilsk and Jinchuan, provided quotations for their cobalt products via websites. Norilsk, in common with the BHP COSS site, also provided information on prices and volumes from recent transactions. As a further development of this type of trading system, Metal Bulletin introduced a Metal Price Watch system in 2004 where buyers and sellers are matched on an open auction system. These prices now form the basis of MB cobalt price quotations. However, two of these price information sources have now disappeared. In August 2008, Norilsk announced it would no longer publish a cobalt price, saying this was giving too much information away to rivals.

chunk.text: disappeared. In August 2008, Norilsk announced it would no longer publish a cobalt price, saying this was giving too much information away to rivals. Furthermore, BHP decided in January 2009 to suspend the publication of the COSS price and the company now states it will direct market all of its cobalt production. In September 2007 Credit Suisse, in co-operation with commodity trader Glencore, launched an over-the-counter (OTC) futures contract for cobalt. This provides investors and other market participants with the opportunity to access the cobalt market in the absence of an exchange. Investors can buy a futures contract for a given month at a quoted price and the transaction is settled against the actual monthly average price quoted by Metal Bulletin. It is purely a cash- settled contract, but does give participants in the physical market the possibility of hedging price risk. Furthermore, Credit Suisse can source liquidity through an alliance with Glencore, which itself has the sales rights to a significant proportion of the world‟s cobalt through agreements with Xstrata and others. Other banks followed suit with Deutsche Bank and Macquarie both offering OTC contracts.

chunk.text: 76 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential 2. The history of cobalt prices 2.1 Prices prior to 1990 Before 1979, all cobalt metal was sold at the producer list price. Producers sold their cobalt in line with the Union Minière or, as it later became, the Gécamines (DRC) price. The so-called “freemarket price” began to emerge between 1978 and 1980, when a supply crisis loosened the producers‟ dominance of the pricing system. During the early 1980s, the free market broadly followed the producer price. In 1984, ZCCM and Gécamines made a bid to recover their price leadership position by restricting supplies in order to keep the price within agreed levels (US$11.45 to US$11.70/lb). At that time, supply and demand were more or less in balance. During 1985, the impact of capacity expansions resulted in production growing much faster than supply and, by the final quarter, stocks had grown to high levels. Furthermore, the freemarket was well supplied through barter deals and producers were selling openly to traders in order to move stocks. The result was a price fall from around US$11 to US$6/lb.

relations.evidence: all cobalt metal was sold at the producer list price

chunk.text: through barter deals and producers were selling openly to traders in order to move stocks. The result was a price fall from around US$11 to US$6/lb. At that time, Gécamines held a key position as the swing supplier. At times of high demand they could meet the additional requirements by selling from their stocks. Conversely, at times of weak demand, Gécamines would stockpile material while their competitors reduced prices to capture marginal market share and ensure they sold their full production. So, when Gécamines opted to sell more material to the market under these conditions in an attempt to capture market share, the price fell rapidly. As prices were still above production costs and cobalt was generally regarded as a by-product, there was little resistance to these falling prices. Only when Gécamines held prices fixed in 1986-87 did the price begin to stabilise. © 2012 CRU International Limited confidential 0 10 20 30 40 50 1986 1988 1990 1992 1994 1996 1998 2000 2002 2004 2006 2008 2010 2012 Nominal freemarket 99.8% price Real freemarket 99.8% price Freemarket prices have seen significant fluctuation US$/lb Data: MB

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 77 CRU International Limited confidential During 1987-90, Gécamines began to look at strategies to maximise its revenues from cobalt. They concluded that to do this in a sustainable way would require a steady increase in cobalt consumption over time. Furthermore, it concluded that this could only be achieved by ensuring stable pricing and secure supplies. Subsequently, the producer price was increased gradually in three iterative steps, with the free market price generally rising in anticipation of these announcements. Meanwhile, Gécamines also sought to establish long-term contracts with consumers in an attempt to persuade them that supplies would be secure and prices would be fixed. To encourage this, the company offered discounts of up to 10% on large tonnages. However, they failed to provide firm guarantees on price structure. As a result, many small and medium-sized consumers preferred to source their material from other producers and purchase at least part of their requirements from traders, in order to manage their exposure to a single supplier.

relations.evidence: During 1987-90, Gécamines began to look at strategies to maximise its revenues from cobalt.

chunk.text: material from other producers and purchase at least part of their requirements from traders, in order to manage their exposure to a single supplier. It was only the large consumers, who purchased more than 100 tonnes of cobalt per year, which accepted the contract terms with Gécamines in order to guarantee their supplies. 2.2 Cobalt prices in 1991-2006 The price of cobalt remained at a relatively low and stable level for the latter years in the 1980s. However by mid-way through 1990 Gécamines began to experience production difficulties. In June 1991, its Kamoto mine, which was an important source of cobalt-rich ore, suffered a serious collapse and production at this location fell to a third of previous levels. This was compounded by the rapidly deteriorating political situation in the DRC (then Zaire). Consumers with long-term contracts began to experience delays in delivery. The market became rife with uncertainty. Doubt was expressed in the media about Gécamines' ability to continue to supply cobalt in the long-term.

chunk.text: ry. The market became rife with uncertainty. Doubt was expressed in the media about Gécamines' ability to continue to supply cobalt in the long-term. Against the backdrop of these supply disruptions the producer price became meaningless and, during 1991, the Zambians saw an opportunity to increase their prices by selling through traders at the freemarket price. Meanwhile, Gécamines was struggling to meet its supply contracts. As a result, all supplies of metal were being priced at freemarket prices, which reached US$30/lb during the early part of 1992, equivalent to a 4-fold increase in little over a year. At the end of 1991, the African producers followed the freemarket price, setting the producer price at US$25/lb. Meanwhile, there emerged an increased availability of supplies of Russian cobalt. Russian material was supplied in ingot form and at a lower grade of purity; 99.3% cobalt compared with 99.8% for Grade "A" cathode. Initially, many consumers were unwilling to take this material as they found the 3kg to 5kg ingots much more difficult to handle than granules or cut cathode.

chunk.text: , many consumers were unwilling to take this material as they found the 3kg to 5kg ingots much more difficult to handle than granules or cut cathode. The continued supply of material from the DRC and Zambia, coupled with increased availability of Russian cobalt, led to a sustained decline in cobalt prices. During June 1992, the freemarket price fell below the producer price and continued falling well into the third quarter of 1993. The African producers were uncertain how best to respond. Faced with a need to keep prices at US$25/lb in order to maintain revenue and the fear of a rapid decline in the metal price similar to that seen in 1985-86, they decided to maintain a higher producer price. The result was that Gécamines and ZCCM lost significant market share to the freemarket. Combined, stocks held by the two companies approached 9,000 tonnes by November 1992.

chunk.text: balt prices have been characterised by strong volatility, with the peaks and troughs seen over the past 18 years correlating with certain key events. The peak of over US$30/lb in 1991 was caused by supply problems from DRC that followed the mine collapse at Kamoto that year and a serious civil disturbance in Katanga Province, where the Gécamines facilities are located. The subsequent trough (US$10/lb) in 1992 reflected a correction in purchasing patterns, partly as a result of high prices and partly because consumers had built up large inventories in the preceding months as they rushed to secure available supply. The next significant peak, in 1996, was the result of steadily increasing demand in a market where supply was tight. This was a period when trading activity was at its most influential. Further to that, prices dropped steadily as more supplies became available. The sharp downward spike in prices in January 1999 to US$7/lb was precipitated by sales of cobalt stock held by ZCCM to a leading trader. There were accusations of price manipulation over this transaction. However the price did not stay at that level for long and returned quite rapidly to over US$20/lb by mid-1999.

chunk.text: ce manipulation over this transaction. However the price did not stay at that level for long and returned quite rapidly to over US$20/lb by mid-1999. Subsequently, increased supply arising from investments made during the high prices in the period 1994-96 led to overcapacity and prices drifted back towards US$7/lb by November 2001. Prices began to rise sharply again in 2003-04 as demand showed significant growth. This stemmed from the vast increase in the amount of rechargeable battery cells required to meet the rapidly growing mobile phone and portable computer markets, and the emergence of China as a major consumer nation. From a peak in early 2004, the price fell gradually through 2004 and 2005, reaching a low point of US$12.50 in February 2006. The fall occurred mainly as a result of a build-up of battery cell inventories and reduced buying from this sector coupled with some aggressive selling by one of the producers. From February 2006 the price rose steadily again, accelerating sharply during the northern hemisphere winter to over US$25/lb in December 2006.

These concerns were compounded by an explosion at the Chambishi furnace in Zambia in January, which deprived the market of around 150 tonnes per month, and ultimately resulted in a loss of 600 tonnes of supply. As a result, prices rose steadily in the first months of 2007, to break through US$30/lb during March, a 10- year high in nominal terms, at that time. A sharp reduction in imports of ores and concentrate from the DRC to China, amid speculation of a very tight supply situation, further contributed to the upward pressure on prices. The DRC government had already deemed the export of unprocessed ores illegal, in an effort to ensure value-adding processing occurred within the country. In addition, strong demand in the rechargeable battery and superalloy sectors ensured that market fundamentals remained tight.

chunk.text: 80 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential As 2007 drew to a close, the forecasts made by some market observers earlier in the year, notably Credit Suisse, that the price would reach a high of US$40/lb for the year were realised and the MB average at the turn of the year (99.8% cobalt) stood at around US$42/lb. Any hope amongst consumers that the market had peaked, at least in the short term, were confounded as prices continued to rise through the first three months of 2008. The year began with a series of brisk price increases as consumers feared an increasingly tight market with demand fundamentals strong and persistent uncertainty over security of supply. For example, reports in late January indicated that electricity blackouts in Zambia had led to reduced output in the country‟s copper belt, affecting operations including Chambishi and Mopani, and leading to fears that supply could not be met in the short term.
