The first indications that the cobalt price would break through the landmark, and historic, US$50/lb level came in February when BHP Billiton raised its bid price on the website to US$51/lb following a series of sales close to that level. The price then peaked at a high of US$52.50/lb for 99.8% material, before falling back to around US$47/lb by the end of May. Prices then entered a period of decline, with a particular drop seen in August. However, this was followed by a sharp increase in September and prices remained relatively high throughout September and October. As November began, prices looked as if they had reached a stable level, though things quickly took a turn for the worse as the full impact of the banking crisis and erosion of economic confidence hit home. Demand started a downward spiral, worsening through Q4 2008 and persisting through Q1 2009. chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 85 CRU International Limited confidential 4.2 Freemarket 99.3% vs. 99.8% quotations Most supply agreements are based on the 99.3% MB quotation, which is considered as the industry benchmark. The spread between the 99.3% and 99.8% quotations is referred to as the premium. The low (99.3%) and high (99.8%) grade markets are two different situations (connected, however), and therefore they each feature their own fundamentals. Hence, changes in the premium indicate market deficits or surpluses and sometimes trigger substitution effects between the two grades. In addition, different end-use sectors refer to different quotations. For instance, the low end of the 99.3% price is the benchmark for chemical production. The more technically demanding consumers, particularly in the superalloys industry have been responsible for increases in the premium. In the past, high premiums required for high grade cathode prompted consumers to substitute the lower grade Russian ingot in many applications that had previously used high grade material.

high grade cathode prompted consumers to substitute the lower grade Russian ingot in many applications that had previously used high grade material. However, during the course of the last ten years, consumers have become more adept at using different varieties of cobalt, thus enhancing their purchasing strategy through greater flexibility and increased bargaining power. The result has been a trend towards a decline in the premium. Since mid-2011, there has been an even faster decline in the premium. In particular since November 2011, the premium has been stabilised within the range of US$0.50-0.75/lb. It would be interesting to analyse how volatile the low and high end prices of each grade are. We conduct this in the period 4 January 2006 to 13 July 2012 by referring to the standard deviation of each series, which is a statistical measure indicating the degree of variation of a set of values around its average. We first examine the 99.8% price. In the above period, the low end averaged US$21.92/lb, with a standard deviation of 9.40. The high end had a mean of US$23.20/lb and a standard deviation of 9.57. We now turn to the 99.3% price.

CRU anticipates that as the LME cobalt trade volumes grow and the contract matures, the degree of convergence between LME and freemarket prices will rise. For fast adjustment between the two series, there are some important factors. Market liquidity is one of them, and this is expected to increase in the cobalt market. Another factor is frequency of trading and price quotations.

88 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential When price data become more frequent, more arbitrage activity is projected to take place in the cobalt market. This has happened in other industrial metals, such as copper. In the case of copper, arbitrage activity between the LME, Comex, and SHFE tends to force prices to converge. When a price window opens, arbitrage activity causes price convergence. However, in the case of cobalt these developments may only happen in the long-run as they are also a function of how financialised a market is. In the foreseeable future, the cobalt market is not predicted to be as financialised as other metal markets are, such as aluminium and copper. From a fundamentals‟ perspective, even though LME cobalt trade has strong potential, there are certain limitations. As the LME is a principal-to-principal market, consumers have not direct access but have to buy metal through a member firm. Furthermore, there are supply limitations. The LME has regulations that producers need to comply with. Then, it is often in the producers‟ interest to sell quality material in the freemarket as they can realise a premium price.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 89 CRU International Limited confidential 5. The cobalt price outlook – medium-term 5.1 Introduction to price forecasting This chapter summarises the approach to forecasting the cobalt price adopted by CRU. We distinguish between the near/medium-term price forecast (2012-2017) and the long-term outlook for cobalt prices. The former is primarily based on the supply and demand fundamentals of the cobalt market, as discussed in Chapter 1 and 2. The long-term price outlook for cobalt follows CRU‟s development of the concept that long-run commodity prices are driven by the long-run marginal costs as well as the industry response to this cost of production by the behaviour of marginal producers and the creation of new capacity. 5.2 The near/medium-term price outlook methodology Over the past 20 years, the use of cobalt has moved from mainly metallurgical applications to a point where some 57% is currently used in chemical sectors. This has meant that refined cobalt is now produced and consumed in both metal and chemical forms.

relations.evidence: The use of cobalt has moved from mainly metallurgical applications to a point where some 57% is currently used in chemical sectors.

chunk.text: ere some 57% is currently used in chemical sectors. This has meant that refined cobalt is now produced and consumed in both metal and chemical forms. CRU believes that developments in cobalt prices are best understood when the fundamentals of both metal and chemical markets are taken into account. However, given the increasing degree of flexibility to switch from one grade to the other, distinguishing between the metallurgical and non-metallurgical sectors may not fully capture market developments. Indeed, deficits in one sector may be met by surpluses in the other, and that affects market prices. As a result, in the 2012 issue we take into consideration the market balance of the total refined market, which consists of both metallurgical and non-metallurgical sectors. This relationship comes from the findings of Chapter 1 and 2, which focused on refined demand and supply respectively. In addition to the refined market balance, we have also taken into consideration the balance at a raw material level. This consists of concentrate and intermediate products to supply the processing operations of cobalt metal and chemical producers who rely on purchased feed.

We do so by estimating the premium as a percentage of the 99.3% price. This averaged 8% in the period 2000 to H1 2012. However, there is a declining trend over time; when we took the average in 2011 to H1 2012, the premium fell to 6%. In H2 2012 it was 4%. Considering the above, we believe that in the forecast period the premium will average 6% of the 99.3% price. CRU believes that estimating the premium as a percentage of the benchmark 99.3% price is a robust methodology to forecast 99.8% prices. Therefore, even when 99.3% prices reach relatively high or low levels, we can still forecast a realistic price for 99.8% cobalt. For instance, when 99.3% prices averaged US$32.57/lb in Q3 2008, the premium of 99.8% was 8% over the 99.3% price. However, in absolute terms the premium was US$2.68/lb. chunk.text: 92 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential 6. The cobalt price outlook – long-term 6.1 The long-term price outlook methodology This section summarises CRU‟s approach to forecasting long-term cobalt prices. Essentially, it follows CRU‟s development of the concept that long-run commodity prices are driven by the long-run marginal cost of production (LRMC), as well as the industry response to this cost of production by the behaviour of marginal producers and the creation of new capacity. In the case of cobalt, developing this concept is more complicated than most other commodities and metals because it is produced in different forms and it is mainly a by-product of copper and nickel, which are more significant metals in terms of volume. 6.2 The long-term market balance Our supply and demand analysis suggests that there is going to be a deficit in the refined cobalt market in the long-run. This immediately implies that cobalt prices will increase in order to attract the development of new projects. With refined demand expected to grow at a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022, sufficient supply will be needed to meet consumption needs.

relations.evidence: long-run commodity prices are driven by the long-run marginal cost of production (LRMC)

chunk.text: ent of new projects. With refined demand expected to grow at a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022, sufficient supply will be needed to meet consumption needs. Our supply and demand balance shows that the refined market will exhibit a deficit of 28,840 tonnes in 2022. We project that the raw material market will be balanced in 2022 but this is only to meet the refined production forecast we present in Chapter 2, rather than world consumption needs. Within the cobalt feed supply analysis, we have assumed that there will be an increase in the supply of concentrates from the DRC in order to meet the cobalt feed needs, in particular those for Chinese processors. That may be affected by the DRC government restricting exports of concentrate in favour of intermediate products and capture domestically the value added. The fact that the raw material market appears to be balanced in 2022 does not mean that there will be enough cobalt units to meet refined demand needs. This is because the refined market will be in deficit, meaning that new processing capacity will be needed, especially in order to produce chemical products.

relations.evidence: the cobalt feed needs, in particular those for Chinese processors

chunk.text: because the refined market will be in deficit, meaning that new processing capacity will be needed, especially in order to produce chemical products. The refined supply and demand analysis conducted in Chapter 1 and 2 shows that the chemical market will be increasingly tighter in the future, and that should trigger expansions or new projects. We believe that this new capacity, in the form of expansions or new projects, will be most likely located in China in order to satisfy soaring demand from regional chemical end-use sectors. The projected refined market deficit in 2022 is an indication of the need of the future cobalt needed at a refined level, on top of current production. These material requirements are expressed in cobalt contained terms at a refined level; they provide a measure of the quantity of cobalt units needed in the supply chain in 2022. Importantly, they do not double count deficits of cobalt units at upstream stages of the supply chain. CRU would like to emphasise at this point that we took a conservative approach when forecasting refined output from current producers in order to deliver a realistic projection.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 93 CRU International Limited confidential 6.3 The long-term cost of production and price forecast CRU follows a simplified approach to assess the LRMC in 2022 based on site and business cost estimates and capital investment for the marginal producers. All values in this section are nominal, referring to 2022 US dollars. As suggested in our fundamentals analysis in the previous section, there is going to be a shortage of cobalt units in the long-run. Our new projects analysis in Chapter 2 shows that the outlook for new projects is uncertain, therefore it is not possible to assess which operations are most likely to add new capacity. Additional capacity can come from a variety of operations, featuring different locations, flow of raw materials and product forms. In addition, finance for those projects may come from different sources, hence affecting the capital charge of each project. As a result, the above factors may potentially generate a broad range of LRMC projections for the cobalt industry. Under these circumstances, defining categorically what the LRMC of the cobalt industry will be is not possible.

Then, we use the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) of the industry, which stands at 9.6%. In addition to that, we have incorporated a country risk premium. That varies, and reflects the degree of risk aversion. This is especially relevant when we assess the different sources of finance. A higher country risk premium results in higher capital charges. When Western companies raise finance for projects in high risk regions, a high country risk premium inflates the capital charge. However, when a Western company with significant exposure to high risk projects needs to raise finance, the country risk premium is lower. In the case of Chinese companies, the country risk premium is low, thus enabling them to have lower capital charges. However, when assessing costs in the long-run, a judgement call is to be made. We believe that the long-run cost level of US$23.58/lb is not a sustainable one, and raw material price negotiations are anticipated to take place. In addition, we believe that costs in the long-run will face downward pressure as a result of integration trends in the cobalt supply chain. This is the reason why we included the main product producers in this long-run cost analysis, even though we anticipate that new capacity will come in the form of the first four types of projects in Table 3.3. chunk.text: n this chapter, our base case price forecast is based on the supply and demand fundamentals for the short/medium-term, and on LRMC for the long-term. The underlying assumptions of the base case are explicitly discussed in Chapter 1 and 2. Our low case scenario is based on a weaker demand outlook and lower inflationary trends. In the short/medium-term, there is more downside rather than upside risk. A weaker price outlook in this case, is mainly affected by less robust demand. But even if the industrial outlook deteriorates rapidly, we still believe that demand for cobalt will be expanding, as analysed in Chapter 1. However, in an oversupplied market, prices could contract further, possibly to a low of US$11.52/lb in 2014. If the economic outlook is stronger than anticipated, we believe that the upside risk in the short/medium-term is limited, impacted by oversupply in the refined market.

As we discussed in the supply and demand balance analysis, the cobalt market is expected to see deficits from 2017 onward. In a tight market, the upside risk is higher than the downside risk. To generate the long-term price forecast we conducted an indicative cost analysis and derived the LRMC of production. Since the long-term price forecast has not been generated by a long- term supply and demand analysis, applying the same high and low case scenarios as in the near/medium-forecast would not be correct. However, looking at the cost structure in Table 3.3, we understand that long-term prices cannot be lower than US$10.00/lb. In the absence of precise information on which projects will proceed, it is impossible to formally define a long- term price range. Overall, it depends on the performance of the global economy and in particular the cobalt-related end-use sectors. 6.5 Upside risks to the price outlook  Stronger than anticipated industrial and economic outlook.

Eurozone malaise takes a further toll The rerun of the Greek election on 17 June changed surprisingly little. It probably did no more than delay Greece’s slow descent into effective default. Meanwhile the malaise is spreading, to Spain, and even to Germany. As a result our Eurozone forecasts remain well below the consensus. Maintaining our bearish stance we’ve downgraded several of our emerging market forecasts, because of first quarter growth in the cases of Brazil and India, and second quarter indicators in China. Elsewhere we’ve trimmed modestly to reflect the debilitating effects of the uncertainty surrounding the Eurozone, which is holding back production plans, investment and hiring at firms globally. Worryingly, the outlook is as clouded as ever by downside risk. Thus the shortcomings of the plan to recapitalise Spain’s banks highlights a greater threat than that of political failure in Greece, because it tells us that contagion is already at work.

relations.evidence: The rerun of the Greek election on 17 June changed surprisingly little. It probably did no more than delay Greece’s slow descent into effective default.

chunk.text: capitalise Spain’s banks highlights a greater threat than that of political failure in Greece, because it tells us that contagion is already at work. The real concern is that events are moving beyond the reach of policy makers. Our downside scenario, to which we attach a probability close to 50%, assumes that the Eurozone’s defences are soon overrun, leading to the chaotic exit of several members, and a crunching recession.  Eurozone-driven uncertainty, compounded by the approaching ‘fiscal cliff’, is sapping corporate confidence in the USA, which in turn is inhibiting hiring and investment, and undermining what little confidence households have. We see this remaining a drag on US growth through 2013 and beyond.  Early in June Spain conceded that its financial sector was in deep crisis and applied for support. Almost inevitably the subsequent promise of €100 bn of funding was sufficiently vague that Spain’s borrowing costs rose to unsustainable rates anyway. The Eurozone – half of which is already in recession – appears locked into a cycle of muddling through at the cost of relentless austerity compounded by unrelenting uncertainty.

chunk.text: third time in four months. The USA’s dividend from rebalancing towards its external sector is diminishing as the crisis in the Eurozone intensifies. This isn’t because of a serious loss of competitiveness, which is still high, although it’s being eroded at the margins by the weakening €, but because of sharp absolute falls in demand and heightened uncertainty. In April the 0.8% m-o-m fall back in exports was concentrated in capital goods and industrial supplies and materials. The main weak spot in the USA’s markets was Europe, where demand for American goods has slumped dramatically on an annualised basis, from almost 15% growth in August 2011 to a contraction of almost 5% in April. In contrast exports to China are still rising. The slump in output is reflected in the labour market. In May payrolls expanded at a very subdued rate for the third month in a row. The increase was revised down from 115,000 in April to 77,000. May hirings only increased by a net 69,000, which edged the unemployment rate back up to 8.2%. Since then jobless claims have continued to trend gently upwards from the lows they hit in February and March.

chunk.text: 102 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 CRU International Limited confidential 2. Regional developments 2.1 The Americas: USA suffers summer doldrums again The indications are that the USA is experiencing its third bout of summer softness in a row. In recent weeks first quarter GDP growth was revised down from 2.2% annualised to 1.9%, thanks to changes in the estimates of the contributions made by trade, stocks and public spending. High frequency data suggests that the second quarter’s performance won’t be much different. We have it at 2.2% annualised, but the risks are that it could come in around 20 basis points lower. For an economy which should be able to grow by 2.5% on trend, and somewhat above that during the recovery phase of its cycle, this is soft. Last year the key problem was the dampening effects on spending caused by high oil prices. This year the problem is mainly to do with the Eurozone’s downswing, and with the debilitating effects of the uncertainty emanating from the Eurozone on confidence. One positive consequence is that inflation is falling back, which should support real spending in the second half of 2012.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 103 CRU International Limited confidential month, are leading firms to delay hiring and investment decisions, which is reflected also in orders for durables. On present evidence this drag is likely to persist through 2012 and into 2013 and beyond. Consumer confidence has taken a knock as a result. The Michigan University index of consumer sentiment dropped to 74.1 in June from 79.3 in May, a six month low, weighed down by a number of real economy factors, but by explicit concerns about conditions in Europe as well. The sogginess in consumer sentiment is reflected in spending. Headline retail sales fell by another 0.2% m-o-m in May, chiefly as a result of lower sales of gasoline. Excluding the three most volatile components of retail sales they inched ahead by a meagre 0.1%. But on some trend measures they are clearly decelerating. In consequence we expect consumer spending to remain subdued through this year, and, as fiscal retrenchment cranks up, through next year as well. In contrast with the Eurozone, however, the recovery in the USA retains a modicum of momentum, despite all the drags.

The recent slump in productivity growth, evident in the 0.9% annualised q-o-q fall in the first quarter, indicates that firms will continue hiring to a degree at least. Indeed, a recent survey of hiring intentions suggested that US firms could step up the pace of job creation in the third quarter. Meanwhile household finances continue to improve gradually. There are signs that house prices are bottoming out, which should draw a line under household wealth, and the ratio of household debt to disposable income is heading back towards its long run value. What’s more, inflation continues to fall, which should provide a real boost to discretionary spending as the year advances. The consumer price index (CPI) fell m-o-m in May by 0.3% which reduced the annual rate of consumer price inflation to 1.7%. The relative resilience of domestic demand is one reason why the Institute of Supply Management’s manufacturing index is still at a reading consistent with around 2% growth.

One of the reasons the US economy retains a degree of buoyancy, which the Eurozone can only dream of, is that policy remains supportive. On the fiscal front that will change early in 2013, but monetary policy is set to stay accommodating well beyond then. The Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) main policy rate is due to stay very low until late 2014. But there’s more: in June the Fed launched a further phase of Operation Twist, whereby it sells short dated Treasury bills out to a maturity of three years and buys long-dated bills of a maturity of six years or more with the proceeds, thereby bearing down on long term yields and interest rates, but without expanding its balance sheet. This operation could continue for another 3-4 months at present rates. Beyond that, the Fed has a further round of quantitative easing (QE) in reserve, especially if the Eurozone’s dominos start to fall.

Despite a raft of stimulus and protectionist measures, economic growth in Brazil has been much weaker than expected in the first half of 2012. Brazil has achieved some success in stimulating job, income and credit growth through these policies but this has largely been through short-term incentives to consumption rather than structural policies aimed at improving investment.

chunk.text: 104 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 CRU International Limited confidential Lately Brazil’s credit-fuelled consumer growth has seemed to be running out of steam, however. Credit creation, for example, decelerated to 1.2% m-o-m in April from 1.9% in March and appears to be slowing spending. Retail sales, for instance, increased by less than 1% m-o-m in April over March. In another indication that domestic consumption has slowed, the consumer price index measure of inflation fell back to below 5%. While the engine of growth in Brazil, domestic consumption, has fallen back other sectors of the economy have also performed weakly. Agricultural output fell sharply, contracting 7.3% q-o-q in the January-March period. In contrast industrial production in the first quarter picked up by 1.7% q-o-q. However, this was in comparison with a very weak prior quarter. The HSBC manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) was unchanged in May at 49.3. This was the second consecutive month of the index in contractionary territory. It has reinforced our view that the manufacturing sector has continued to struggle of late. Investment in infrastructure would certainly help Brazil.

chunk.text: It has reinforced our view that the manufacturing sector has continued to struggle of late. Investment in infrastructure would certainly help Brazil. Currently Brazil’s total investment is less than 19% of GDP, one of the lowest ratios among emerging economies. Furthermore, Brazil spends only 2% of GDP on infrastructure, compared with 10% in China. The reason for this is that high interest rates have attracted foreign capital and driven up the value of the Brazilian real, which has made manufacturing and services uncompetitive and dampened investment. The Brazilian central bank is currently in a monetary loosening cycle which should help keep at bay some foreign inflows and this is expected to continue to be the policy stance for the immediate future. As a result of the worse than expected first quarter and poor high frequency data into the second quarter, we now forecast growth to be slower in 2012 than 2011 and have revised down our outlook for growth this year to 2.4% (from 3.1% last month). However, we expect the impact of loose fiscal and monetary policies on private and government consumption to push growth higher in 2013.

The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 105 CRU International Limited confidential The result of the Greek election on 17 June, although broadly viewed as the best possible outcome in that it averted an imminent Greek exit from the Eurozone, has not led us to change our view that Greece will eventually have to default. The new national unity government is likely to face major problems. Pushing a consensus line on how to tackle the crisis will be difficult, as the coalition is formed of three disparate parties, coming together due to a shared fear of the catastrophic impact of a Greek exit from the euro. They also have different ideologies on how to return Greece to economic growth. The Syriza party will take up the position of opposition, and with the ever-present issues of austerity-fatigue and the failing Greek economy, the new government is likely to be under permanent attack. There is also the threat of new elections always on the horizon if one of the coalition partners drops out – a likely outcome even before the year-end. Furthermore, a renegotiation of the bailout terms will, at best, only buy the new government some extra time.

And despite some tinkering with the agreement (slightly lower interest rates; extending the debt repayment period; possibly even easing the deficit targets slightly and stumping up some funds for investment), the core aims will ultimately stay the same. This means the agreement will remain unachievable, ineffective and poorly targeted on the real problems in Greece (namely competitiveness and systemic administration flaws). Uncertainty will continue to prevail. The controversial opposition will benefit as the government struggles with the adjustment programme. Furthermore, as Greece approaches a balanced budget and a more stable banking sector (though still messy), an exit will look increasingly attractive – particularly if the alternative for Athens is to permanently give up economic and political sovereignty. chunk.text: 106 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 CRU International Limited confidential but with policymakers doing enough to limit the worst of the contagion effects of this event. Meanwhile, apart from the election in Greece, the focus in recent weeks has been on Spain. The agreement of a €100 bn bailout of Spanish banks (via the Spanish government) is likely to provide much needed assistance to a sector that is struggling under the weight of a deteriorating economy, a crippled housing market, and massive capital outflows. However, the financial market response has been muted because of fears that the new loans will rank above existing Spanish bonds in the case of a default (therefore investors would demand higher yields to compensate), and that the loans will simply not be large enough. Yields on 10-year government debt remain close to the critical 7% level which is deemed unsustainable. These pressures make it more likely that the European Central Bank (ECB) will cut interest rates at its next meeting in July. However, this may well have a limited impact, since interbank rates are already low.

Furthermore, plans for the Eurozone bail-out fund to purchase sovereign debt look unlikely to have a lasting effect on governments’ borrowing costs, unless accompanied by a significant increase in firepower. In terms of the real economy, the uncertainty surrounding the crisis is continuing to have detrimental effects. The composite PMI was unchanged at 46.0 in June, and therefore remains well below the 50 ‘no-change’ reading and points to a second quarter fall in GDP of almost 0.5%. Furthermore, April’s 0.8% m-o-m fall (2.3% y-o-y) in industrial production provides a further sign that the Eurozone’s narrow escape from technical recession in the first quarter will not last very long. Needless to say, the peripheral economies are seeing the biggest falls in production (Spain -8.3% y-o-y, Italy -9.2%), but even Germany’s super-competitive industrial sector has contracted by 0.8% over the last year.

Our baseline projection still assumes that there will be a disorderly Greek default sometime in the near future (although the relatively positive outcome of the Greek election may have pushed this date into 2013). We also assume further bail-outs for Ireland and Portugal, as well as some assistance for Spain and Italy (most likely to control bond yields via debt purchases) coming from the stability fund. The contagion effects of a Greek default will be significant, although we still assume the ECB, under the direction of Germany, will intervene to limit the worst of these effects. This could involve full blown quantitative easing by the ECB, which so far has been opposed by Germany on the grounds of inflationary effects and moral hazard, or more significant moves to the idea of a fiscal union via the issuance of Eurobonds. Under these assumptions, we retain our below consensus Eurozone GDP forecast of around 1% contraction this year, and stagnation in 2013.

Meanwhile, Germany is increasingly showing signs that it is not immune to problems in the rest of the continent. Amidst a climate of uncertainty, business investment is weak and exports to the rest of the continent, as well as to Asia are slowing. The headline ZEW index of investor confidence plunged in June from +10.8 to a five-month low of -16.9, the sharpest monthly decline since October 1998. Furthermore, the German PMI fell for a fifth month running, from 49.3 to 48.5, suggesting that at least some of the first quarter’s 0.5% rise in GDP will be reversed this quarter. Finally, June’s Ifo survey reinforced this message, with the index falling to its lowest level since March 2010. Overall, we expect growth of 0.6% in Germany this year, and 1.1% in 2013.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 107 CRU International Limited confidential In the UK, recent problems have been concentrated in the manufacturing sector, stemming from a sharp drop in orders, both from home and abroad. Service sector firms have also reported a notable deterioration in sentiment following the latest developments in the Eurozone. Looking ahead, growth will be bumpy because of the impact of the Diamond Jubilee and the Olympic games. Falling inflation should help give consumers some extra spending power; however the risks from the Eurozone crisis continue to overshadow any positive developments. We now forecast stagnation this year, and growth of 1.5% in 2013. In Russia, survey readings have remained buoyant this year with the labour market also remaining fairly strong. However, the economy is at risk from the recent slide in oil prices, considering half of budget revenues come from oil and gas. We now forecast growth of 4.1% this year and 2.9% in 2013. Central Europe has not fared so well this year, with the Eurozone crisis sapping confidence in the region.

Japan’s recovery appeared to be losing some momentum since the first quarter but activity remains on track to improve at a moderate pace. GDP growth of 1.2% q-o-q over the first three months of the year was thanks to strong base effects and temporary boosts to consumption. However, the first signs are emerging that these effects are beginning to wane. The surge in private consumption, buoyed up in the first quarter by temporary government incentive schemes, may have started to fade. The schemes generated strong retail sales in January and February but sales contracted in March and April, suggesting that some payback has already started. Thus the abrupt departure of Greece, Ireland, Portugal, Spain and Italy from the Eurozone is the focus of this scenario. For convenience we assume that it begins in the third quarter of 2012. What might a Eurozone crash look like? The restored local currencies of the exiting countries would depreciate sharply, we assume by 50% against the euro in Greece, by 25% in Italy, Spain and Portugal, and by 15% in Ireland. There would be extensive collateral damage to asset prices. Thus equity prices could fall by 30% initially in Greece and remain 10% below base case levels through to the end of 2016. In the other exiting countries equities might fall by 20% at the onset of the crisis and still be 8% below our base by the end of our forecast period. Elsewhere in the bloc equities could fall by 15% initially, but subsequently recover as the stronger northern Eurozone economies returned to growth. The damage wouldn’t be limited to Europe, however.

Meanwhile the banking sectors in the exiting countries and in the surviving rump of the Eurozone would suffer heavy losses and tighten credit standards dramatically. These losses would imply the socialisation of significant additional debt. As a result spreads on government bonds would rise by 1,200 bp in Greece and 1,100 bp in Portugal, Italy and Spain in the early stages of the crisis. Business and consumer confidence would deteriorate sharply. The euro might initially depreciate against the $ by 15%. As the surviving rump of the Eurozone stabilised, the euro would begin appreciating, because of the inherent structural strength of the northern European economies, and it could end up end up around 15% stronger against the US dollar. As a result the ECB would be likely to cut its policy rate to zero and keep it there until mid 2016.

chunk.text: 128 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 CRU International Limited confidential 4. Industrial outlook 2011-2016 4.1 The motor vehicle sector: leading manufacturing higher 4.1.1 USA The auto sector remains a bright spot in the US economy, with the turnaround set to more than offset the expected declines in Europe and Asia. US production of motor vehicles and parts surged for the third consecutive quarter in the first three months of this year (around 14% y-o-y) and output continued to grow in April. The initial recovery from supply chain disruptions appears to have given way to strong demand from consumers for new vehicle purchases. Having postponed spending during the downturn and amid ongoing uncertainty about the recovery, consumers appear to be replacing ageing cars to benefit from greater fuel economy. As a result of the increase in demand and with lean inventories, car makers including GM, Ford Motor Co and Chrysler have ramped up production schedules. We forecast total US vehicle production to grow by 11% y-o-y this year as a result. Sales have benefited from declines in fuel costs, new products and the improved availability of cars from Japan.

relations.evidence: US production of motor vehicles and parts surged for the third consecutive quarter in the first three months of this year (around 14% y-o-y) and output continued to grow in April.

chunk.text: by 11% y-o-y this year as a result. Sales have benefited from declines in fuel costs, new products and the improved availability of cars from Japan. 4.1.2 Europe In contrast with the USA and Japan, European car manufacturing and sales have not performed well. Western European vehicle production fell by 7% y-o-y in the first quarter and we expect it to continue declining throughout 2012. We forecast auto production in France, Germany, Italy, Spain and the UK to collectively contract by around 8.6% y-o-y this year. Meanwhile, production in Europe is a mixed picture. German and UK markets are holding up well, but the Italian and Spanish markets are projected to remain particularly weak. The Eurozone debt crisis continued to weigh on consumer confidence and as a result car sales in Europe have tumbled, falling by almost 10% y-o-y in May. Sales in Germany and the UK held up well until May when they joined other markets and suffered a drop in sales volumes. The upshot is that we forecast the Western Europe motor vehicle sector as a whole to be weak this year with sales falling by around 6%, and the fragility should continue into 2013.

While some of the pick-up in growth can be attributed to base effects from low sales last year, the overall picture is encouraging given the recent weakening of the economy. According to the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers, auto sales in the first five months of 2012 totalled 8 M units and growth may intensify should authorities reintroduce subsidies on purchases of new fuel-efficient cars. Meanwhile vehicle production gathered momentum in April, after several weak months, to expand by almost 11% y-o-y. In Japan, domestic sales growth of 94% y-o-y and production growth of 174% y-o-y in April were distorted by last year’s earthquake and subsequent supply chain disruptions. Nonetheless, the auto sector appeared to bounce back as Japanese firms looked to regain lost market share.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 131 CRU International Limited confidential 4.2 The construction sector: few bright spots 4.2.1 USA Recent data appears to show that the US housing sector may be stabilising, and there may even be some grounds for modest optimism. Construction has had a lift from the rise in sales of new homes which picked-up by 3.3% m-o-m in April. Housing starts, for example, rose by 2.6% m-o-m in the same month, albeit from a very low base. The pickup in home construction and commercial projects offset a fifth consecutive decline in public sector builds. In an encouraging sign of future activity the number of permits for residential construction rose by almost 8% m-o-m in May. What’s more, US homebuilder sentiment, as measured by the National Association of Home Builders/Wells Fargo, was at its highest for five years in June, an important month for the sector to record a more buoyant outlook given that a significant amount of construction takes place over the summer. We forecast total construction to bounce back to around 5.6% growth this year and to average around 7% annual growth out to 2016.

We forecast total construction to bounce back to around 5.6% growth this year and to average around 7% annual growth out to 2016. 4.2.2 Europe Construction output in the EU rose by 12% m-o-m in March, but building activity in the public sector is still significantly down in y-o-y terms. Germany made the most gains, with construction increasing by over 6% m-o-m, while the European periphery economies recorded the largest contractions, notably Spain which shrank by almost 11%. Germany’s construction sector made a solid start to 2012 largely thanks to a relatively healthy economy and a robust labour market. We forecast total construction to grow by just over 1% this year, driven by slightly stronger growth in residential building. Our outlook is for non-residential building to pick up markedly in 2013 and beyond. Elsewhere in Europe, we forecast all segments of construction activity to lose ground this year with total construction activity in Italy this year to contract by around 2.5%. In Spain the situation is forecast to be even worse with public spending related construction not expected to return to growth before 2015.

The risks in the European forecast are heavily skewed to the downside, especially in the event of chaotic defaults, through public sector expenditure cuts and the withdrawal of private sector access to credit hitting construction disproportionately. 4.2.3 Asia Although emerging markets are less exposed to the fiscal constraints of the developed economies, in some emerging Asian economies there is growing evidence of a moderation in the growth of construction activity. In China, for example, housing sales struggled in the first quarter and property starts fell, but investment remained firm as property developers rushed to get projects on the market. That said, completions are not expected to sell as strongly as in 2011 and elevated inventories are likely to translate into more affordable housing. Meanwhile, demand for reconstruction-related building in Japan has soared since the natural disasters of last year.

Construction orders were 25% y-o-y higher in March and private residential starts were up 20% y-o-y but base effects help to explain the strong rebounds in these sectors. Our outlook is for around 3% growth in construction this year but is tempered by tight credit conditions acting as a restraint on business investment.
